verbal.questionText: Which of the following best tells what this passage is about?

answerChoices.text: Unlike their predecessors, current citizens of Moscow are taking a respectful approach to modernizing their 850-year-old city, allowing new building while preserving important structures and artifacts from the past.

The architecture of Moscow is a strange mix of styles, brought about by chaotic periods in its history during which new structures were built on top of old, or else older structures were simply destroyed.

Today, approximately 5,000 active archeological sites in Moscow are being preserved by the Department of Preservation of Historical Monuments, and every day archeologists are making new and important discoveries.
Because its architectural treasures are now being preserved, Moscow is faring much better than it did when it was part of the Soviet Union and under communist rule.
Manege Square is a good example of what archaeologists and community members can do to satisfy preservation societies.

verbal.questionText: The passage states that Moscow's Manege Square has been and most likely will continue to be a

answerChoices.text: residential area.
community meeting place.
business district.
government center.
cultural museum.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following can be inferred from the information in the second paragraph?

answerChoices.text: Historically, the people of Moscow were more interested in modernization than in preservation.
The Soviet government destroyed many old buildings, in keeping with an anti-czarist policy.
There are very few 850-year-old cities in existence and fewer yet that preserve their past.
Moscow has a history of invasions, with each new conqueror destroying the buildings of the previous regime.
Russia officially became a nation in 1147.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following assumptions is most likely a viewpoint that would be expressed by the writer of this passage?

answerChoices.text: Generally speaking, people should be more interested in building new structures than in saving old ones.
Architectural history has little meaning to people struggling to form a new government.
Progress and preservation are equally important principles of urban planning.
Archaeologists and bureaucrats generally do not work well together.
Architectural function is more important than architectural style.

verbal.questionText: According to the passage, which of the following is true of archaeologists in Moscow?

answerChoices.text: They have uncovered a number of significant items that will help them to understand Moscow's history.
They operate under severe time constraints, as contractors wait to begin new buildings.
There are not nearly enough archaeological teams to conduct all the possible research.
They are primarily concerned with preserving the artifacts of modes of transportation.
They are opposed to any more new construction in Manege Square.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following best tells what this passage is about?

answerChoices.text: how and why a special monument was built
why a city needs a monument for its skyline
how monuments serve to inspire mankind
why people build monuments to their past
In one day, six thousand visitors can visit the top of the arch.

verbal.questionText: Who chose Saarinen's design for the arch?

answerChoices.text: a team of St. Louis architects
the Jefferson National Expansion Memorial Association
Eero Saarinen's engineering partners
the National Park Service
It was chosen by popular vote.

verbal.questionText: The author compares the arch to the Great Pyramid in Egypt in order to

answerChoices.text: show readers that the arch was more difficult to construct.
convince readers that the arch is more beautiful than the Great Pyramid.
make readers aware of how much time it took to build the arch.
prove that modern architects equal those in ancient times.
give readers some idea of the height of the arch.

verbal.questionText: You can tell that the author probably considers the arch to be

answerChoices.text: amusing.
old-fashioned.
impressive.
silly.
terrifying.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following best tells what this passage is about?

answerChoices.text: Thomas is having difficulty writing a song until his growling stomach provides him with a rhythm and a theme.
Hungry and tired, Thomas opens his refrigerator but discovers that he doesn't have any food.
After dinner, Thomas writes two new songs about life on the reservation.
Thomas goes to sleep watching The Sound of Music on television.
Listening to the faint voices that echo all over the reservation, Thomas finds it easy to come up with a new song.

verbal.questionText: Based on the passage, we can conclude that Thomas

answerChoices.text: does not take good care of himself.
is poor.
has always wanted to be in a band.
is waiting for someone to help him.
watches too much television.

verbal.questionText: Thomas titles the song "Reservation Blues." Based on this passage, you can expect the song to be about

answerChoices.text: the good times he's had on the reservation.
how he and his friends started a band.
fry bread.
the sounds he hears at night on the reservation.
the difficulties of living on a reservation.

verbal.questionText: Why does Thomas keep opening and closing the refrigerator?

answerChoices.text: He keeps hoping food will magically appear.
He can't believe that the refrigerator is empty.
He is angry and wants the door to break off.
He likes the noise the door makes.
He is bored.

verbal.questionText: The narrator tells us that "Thomas was hungry on a reservation where there are ninety-seven different ways to say fry bread." What is the purpose of this sentence?

answerChoices.text: to show us how important fry bread is to the language
to show us how hungry Thomas was
to make us want to try fry bread
to show us the irony of the situation
to show us how Thomas was inspired

verbal.questionText: Which of the following best tells what this passage is about?

answerChoices.text: Scientists have strengthened the previously weak alkaline battery.
Battery-driven devices are replacing common household fixtures.
The alkaline battery has affected the development and marketing of portable devices.
This is how alkaline batteries are produced and manufactured.
American homes can reduce the amount of electricity they use.

verbal.questionText: As compared with batteries made after 1959, batteries made before 1959

answerChoices.text: were weaker and had a shorter life span.
had more power but a shorter life span.
were created for use in household appliances.
were made without cathodes and anodes.
did not use an electrolyte.

verbal.questionText: The passage suggests that alkaline batteries create electricity by

answerChoices.text: passing positive and negative electrodes through a zinc electrolyte.
passing electrons between manganese dioxide and carbon.
exchanging the negative cathode with a positive one.
transforming negative electrodes into anodes.
passing electrons between the cathode and the anode.

verbal.questionText: What made researchers in the late 1950s decide that batteries needed to be improved?

answerChoices.text: Batteries were weaker and had a shorter life span.
Batteries had more power but a shorter life span.
Batteries were created for use in household appliances.
Batteries were made without cathodes and anodes.
Batteries did not use an electrolyte.

verbal.questionText: The last paragraph states that batteries will be smaller and more powerful in the future. This suggests that

answerChoices.text: research for improving batteries is currently under way.
batteries will replace electricity as a primary power source.
household appliances will also be smaller.
portable appliances will eventually become obsolete.
batteries will require increased amounts of alkaline.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following best tells what this passage is about?

answerChoices.text: Old friends, Jorge and the narrator are riding to school together as usual.
The narrator expects to become close friends with his neighbor Jorge.
The narrator is annoyed with Jorge's driving and will not ride with him again.
It's clear that the narrator and Jorge take turns driving each other to school.
Jorge is driving the narrator to school, but neither of them is very comfortable.

verbal.questionText: What event probably occurred before the beginning of this excerpt?

answerChoices.text: Jorge drove the narrator to school for several days.
The narrator's sister recovered from her illness.
Jorge asked whether he has a silly laugh.
The narrator asked Jorge for a ride to school.
The narrator noticed road construction on the way to school.

verbal.questionText: Why does the narrator lie to Jorge in the opening lines of this excerpt? The narrator

answerChoices.text: wants to avoid the awkwardness that would come from telling the truth.
hopes to become close friends with Jorge.
is a compulsive liar who is unable to distinguish between lies and the truth.
plans to tell Jorge the truth about his laugh when they reach the school.
wants to punish Jorge for his poor jokes.

verbal.questionText: The title of this story is "Discomfort and Joy." What does this title suggest will happen between the narrator and Jorge?

answerChoices.text: The tension between them will probably explode into violence.
The tension between them will be resolved, leading to a better relationship between the narrator and Jorge.
The tension between them will continue long after the ride in the car.
The tension between them will increase steadily as the car gets closer to the school.
The tension between them will turn out to have been in the narrator's imagination all the time.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following best tells what this passage is about?

answerChoices.text: Some animals seem to instinctively display signs of high intelligence.
An amazing parrot can classify materials; classifying is a basic thinking skill.
Chimpanzees have been known to obtain termites to eat by using a complex series of actions.
Some animals can use human language to communicate.
New research shows more signs of intelligence in animals than was previously thought.

verbal.questionText: Crows use sticks to pry peanuts out of cracks. Which of the following is the kind of intelligence or conditioning the situation describes?

answerChoices.text: rote learning
tools
communication
instinct
cueing

verbal.questionText: According to the passage, animal intelligence has been indicated by

answerChoices.text: success in the rote conditioning of many animals.
observation of animals in the wild.
observation of animals in zoos.
a new study of instinctive animal behavior.
observation of animals using tools.

verbal.questionText: The concluding paragraph of this passage implies which of the following?

answerChoices.text: There is no definitive line between those animals with intelligence and those without.
Animals are being given opportunities to display their intelligence.
Research showing higher animal intelligence may fuel debate on ethics and cruelty.
Animals are capable of untrained thought well beyond mere instinct.
Animals are capable of acting intelligently based on instinct.

verbal.questionText: According to the passage, which of the following is true about animals communicating through the use of human language?

answerChoices.text: Parrots can imitate or repeat a sound.
Dolphins click and whistle.
Crows screech warnings to other crows.
Chimpanzees and gorillas have been trained to use sign language.
Animals seem to communicate with each other.

verbal.questionText: According to the passage, what conclusion can be reached about the chimpanzee's ability to use a tool to get food?

answerChoices.text: It illustrates instinct because he is able to get his food and eat it.
It illustrates intelligence because termites are protein-packed.
It illustrates instinct because he faced a difficult task and accomplished it.
It illustrates rote learning because he stored knowledge away and called it up at the right time.
It illustrates high intelligence because it is a complex task that calls upon stored knowledge.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following is NOT a sign of animal intelligence?

answerChoices.text: shows insight
follows cues
uses tools
makes a plan
classifies objects

verbal.questionText: Which of the following best tells what this passage is about?

answerChoices.text: The islands along the coastline of Maine enchant residents and visitors alike. Visitors can receive nature instruction from Arcadia park naturalists.
Only wealthy summer vacationers who have homes in Bar Harbor ever visit Maine's beautiful Mt. Desert Island.
The tops of a former mountain range became islands off the Maine coast.
Marine fossils found on Mt. Desert Island were 225 feet above sea level; that was the level of the shoreline prior to the glacier.
Natural forces formed Maine coastal islands; today's Mt. Desert Island is a beautiful place, popular with residents and tourists.

verbal.questionText: According to the passage, the large number of small islands along the coast of Maine are the result of

answerChoices.text: glaciers forcing a mountain range into the sea.
Maine's location between the temperate and sub-Arctic zones.
the irregularity of the Maine coast.
the need for summer communities for wealthy tourists and artists.
the incredibly long coastline.

verbal.questionText: The content of the fourth paragraph indicates that the writer believes that

answerChoices.text: the continued existence of national parks is threatened by budget cuts.
the best way to preserve the environment on Mt. Desert Island is to limit the number of visitors.
national parks allow large numbers of people to visit and learn about interesting wilderness areas.
Mt. Desert Island is the most interesting tourist attraction in Maine.
Mt. Desert Island should be closed to tourists in order to preserve its delicate ecological system.

verbal.questionText: According to the selection, the coast of Maine is

answerChoices.text: 2,500 miles long.
3,500 miles long.
225 miles long.
235 miles long.
522 miles long.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following statements best expresses the main idea of the fourth paragraph of the selection?

answerChoices.text: The wealthy residents of Mt. Desert Island selfishly kept it to themselves.
Acadia National Park is one of the smallest of the national parks.
On Mt. Desert Island, there is great tension between the year-round residents and the summer tourists.
Due to its location and environment, Mt. Desert Island supports an incredibly diverse animal and plant life.
Many migrating birds rest on the island.

verbal.questionText: The answer to which of the following questions would best tell what this passage is about?

answerChoices.text: How did scientists apply what they knew about storms to their research?
What made scientists choose Goodland, Kansas, as their research base?
How did the data collected on land compare to that collected in the air?
What is the difference between a thunderstorm and a lightning storm?
How did scientists attempt to learn more about severe storm systems?

verbal.questionText: Which of the following actions did the scientists carry out first?

answerChoices.text: They gathered storm data.
They formed a hypothesis.
They drove toward the storm front.
They waited for bad weather.
They measured water particles.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following is suggested about the research data collected by the scientists in Kansas?

answerChoices.text: The data has explained why some storms dump hail and others do not.
The data was so great that it will take time to sort out properly.
The data has provided clear answers to the scientists' questions.
The data will trigger other research projects at the STEPS laboratories.
The data contradicted what most researchers suspected about super cells.

verbal.questionText: The passage states that large, chaotic storm systems can be caused

answerChoices.text: by tornadoes.
by large hail and ice.
when water particles make clouds grow too large.
when barometric pressure rises and humidity lowers.
when moist air and hot, dry air clash.

verbal.questionText: Some of the scientists rode in a special single-engine plane mainly because they

answerChoices.text: wanted to view the storms from another angle.
needed to communicate with the researchers on the ground.
hoped to gather data about the composition of storm clouds.
were the only ones who could operate the special radar station.
hoped to be able to see exactly how hail is formed.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following best tells what this passage is about?

answerChoices.text: Jane finds the idea of the world beyond Lowood frightening and wonders if she can ever leave.
Looking at the blue mountain peaks, Jane longs to climb them.
Comparing the world of Lowood with what she knows of the outside world, Jane is eager to leave the boarding school.
Jane remembers vacations away from school and decides she will leave.
Jane considers a letter from a friend urging her to leave Lowood.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following best describes life at Lowood?

answerChoices.text: very unconventional and modern
very structured and isolated
harsh and demeaning
liberal and carefree
urban and sophisticated

verbal.questionText: The narrator

answerChoices.text: realizes she wants to experience the world.
decides that she must get married.
realizes she can never leave Lowood.
decides to return to her family.
determines to become a teacher.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following best describes Jane's nature?

answerChoices.text: fearful and angry
timid and solitary
dependent on her friends
steady and sensible
curious and adventurous

verbal.questionText: The narrator reduces her prayer from freedom to "at least a new servitude." Judging from the passage, which of these is most likely the reason that she changes her prayer?

answerChoices.text: She doesn't believe in prayer.
She is not in a free country.
She has been offered a position as a servant.
She thinks that freedom might be an unrealistic wish.
She has been treated like a slave at Lowood.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following best tells what this passage is about?

answerChoices.text: a suggestion that there are unknown species of sage grouse
a comparison and contrast of two species of sage grouse
a plea to save Colorado's endangered sage grouse
a theory about sage grouse that has been proven to be true
a prediction that new sage grouse species will soon be discovered

verbal.questionText: Based on the passage, how are the Gunnison sage grouse and the northern sage grouse alike?

answerChoices.text: They have the same coloration.
The two species interbreed.
They are similar in size and weight.
Their populations have been declining.
They have the same strut and the same tail feathers.

verbal.questionText: The passage suggests that the Gunnison sage grouse has probably existed

answerChoices.text: ever since DNA tests demonstrated it was distinct.
as long as any other species of sage grouse.
ever since the end of the nineteenth century.
as long as there has been an endangered species list.
ever since people settled the Gunnison Basin.

verbal.questionText: Which of the following proved that the Gunnison sage grouse and the northern sage grouse were different species?

answerChoices.text: Taxonomists cited the birds' different sizes.
Dr. Young's studies revealed that the birds had different tail feathers.
DNA testing indicated that the birds were not closely related.
The Gunnison sage grouse was put on the endangered species list.
The birds' populations showed markedly different rates of decline.

verbal.questionText: The discussion of Dr. Young's research suggests that

answerChoices.text: mating only occurs within, and not among, sage grouse species.
female sage grouse do not recognize male mating calls.
sage grouse mating habits explain why the species are endangered.
Gunnison sage grouse have a mating call but northern sage grouse do not.
DNA has little or nothing to do with species' mating habits.

math.questionText: The crown in the figure is made up of toothpicks that each have the same length. If each toothpick is 6 centimeters long, the base of the crown uses two toothpicks, and the three points use one toothpick on each side, what is the perimeter of the crown in centimeters?

Gary has a collection of 16 different movies, and his roommate Parth has a collection of 18 different movies. If Parth and Gary have 4 movies common to both collections, how many different movies do they have between them?

A model sailboat is floating on the water and attached to the dock using a 1 meter long string, as shown in the figure. If the string is tied to a post on the edge of the side of the dock, which of the following best shows the area of water on which the sailboat can float?

answerChoices.text: (image A)
(image B)
(image C)
(image D)
(image E)

math.questionText: At a party, there are exactly 4 times as many adults as children. Which of the following could be the total number of people at this party?
Using a pair of scissors, which of the following CANNOT be made from a 20 cm by 28 cm rectangular sheet of paper by one straight cut?

answerChoices.text: triangle
square
trapezoid
pentagon
hexagon

math.questionText: According to the graph, what was the average number of students taking the swimming class during the four months of March through June?
Questions 58-59 are based on the following definition. For all real numbers n and r, n ♣ r = (n - 1) - n/r. What is the value of 4 ♣ 2?
Using the definition n ♣ r = (n - 1) - n/r: If Q ♣ 2 = 4, then Q =
If Henry traveled at a rate of 45 miles per hour, how many hours did it take him to drive 225 miles?
Raoul wants to leave a 20 percent tip for a dinner that costs $20.95. Which of the following is closest to the amount of tip he should leave?

Juan studied from 4:00 p.m. to 6:00 p.m. and finished one-third of his assignments. He is taking a break and wants to finish his homework by 10:30 p.m. If he plans to continue working at the same rate, what is the latest that he can return to his studies?
Mrs. Brown and her c children each ate four strawberries. What is the total number of strawberries they ate?

answerChoices.text: 4c

math.questionText: Which figure can be drawn WITHOUT lifting the pencil or retracing?

answerChoices.text: (triangle figure)
(rectangle with diagonal figure)
(square with X figure)
(diamond figure)
(circle with line figure)

math.questionText: If 0.59 is about N/5, then N is closest to which of the following?
If the largest of seven consecutive integers is 25, what is the average of the seven integers?
The price of a bag of pretzels increased from $0.93 to $1.08. The increase in price is closest to what percent?
The result of 7,236 divided by 89.2 is closest to which of the following?
What is the least number of square tiles with side 6 cm needed to cover a rectangular floor 72 cm long and 48 cm wide?
It takes Amari 5 minutes to type p pages. At this rate, how many minutes will it take him to type 20 pages?

answerChoices.text: 4p

math.questionText: The width of a rectangular swimming pool is one-quarter of its length. If the length is 60 meters, what is the perimeter of the pool?
The price of a dress at a clothing store decreases by 20 percent every month it is not sold. After 3 months, the current price of the unsold dress is approximately what percent of the original price?
If p is a positive integer and n is a negative integer, which of the following is greatest?

answerChoices.text: p/n
n/p
It cannot be determined from the information given.

math.questionText: At a party, 2/3 of the guests drank only soda and 1/4 of the guests drank only juice. If the remaining 5 guests had nothing to drink, then how many guests were at the party?
What is the greatest prime number less than 40?
If 3(0.4 + 0.07) is equal to 6 times a number, what is the number?
What is the least integer greater than 27/4?
What is the value of the following expression? (7/2 - 1/4) / (2/3 - 1/4)
Linda needs to read 14 pages for her History class, 26 pages for English, 12 pages for Civics, and 28 pages for Biology. She has read 1/6 of the entire number of pages. How many pages has she read?

answerChoices.text: 80 pages
13 1/3 pages
48 1/2 pages
17 pages
21 pages
